steps.text: Determine the source moisture concentration to select the appropriate sampling train configuration.
Collect gas samples from a sampling manifold using evacuated canisters equipped with a critical orifice.
Measure stack carbon dioxide (CO2) concentration.
Measure water and CO2 in conjunction with stack or duct volumetric flows.
Identify and quantify VFCs in gas samples from canisters by GC/MS.

preconditions.text: Staff must be trained in the best practices for extracting representative gas samples, conditioning the gas if necessary, and collecting the gas sample.
Perform confirmatory analysis (blanking) of cleaned passivated canisters before they are sent to the field for sampling.

constraints.text: Canisters must be passivated silicon ceramic lined stainless-steel containers, or equivalent, able to be pressurized to at least 2 atmospheres.
Gas samples from stationary combustion sources contain significant quantities of CO2 and condensed water or water vapor which interfere with VFC compounds in this method.

Water in the sample gas was found to coelute with analytes on certain chromatography columns used to evaluate this method and affected the response of those analytes. Poor or inconsistent water management during preconcentration can cause peak broadening and retention time shifts.
Acid gases and water condensed in canisters can result in corrosion of the interior surface of the canisters, resulting in reactive surfaces that compromise target compounds.
CO2 in the collected sample may coelute and/or interfere with the quantitation of target compounds in this method.
If coeluting compounds have common ions, the common ions should not be used as the target’s quantifying or qualifying ions.

steps.text: Cool the GC oven to sub-ambient temperatures at the start of the chromatographic run.

preconditions.text: Depending on the choice of column (e.g., film thickness) and the volatility of the target compounds, to allow resolution of very volatile organic compounds.

constraints.text: Carrier gas lines supplying the GC must be constructed from clean stainless-steel or copper tubing with non-polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) thread sealants.
Flow controllers, cylinder regulators, or other pneumatic components fitted with rubber components are not suitable.

steps.text: Configure the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management by connecting a heated transfer probe, a heated filter, an impinger conditioning system, an unheated transfer line, and the unheated critical orifice and connecting fittings to a bypass pump.
Soak glassware (including impingers, stems, and caps) and filter frits in hot soapy water (Alconox® or equivalent) at 50 °C or higher.
Rinse the glassware and filter frits three times with hot tap water.
Rinse the glassware and filter frits three times with DI water.
Rinse the glassware and filter frits three times with methanol.

preconditions.text: Stack moisture is >3% v/v or acid gas levels are a concern.
Clean glassware and sampling apparatus thoroughly before use.

steps.text: Prepare the Direct VFC Sampling System.
Connect the sample probe liner to the heated glass filter housing with a QFF or GFF.
Connect the exit of the heated glass filter housing to the impinger conditioning system.
Connect the appropriate transfer line in series with the critical orifice tee and the tubing to the bypass pump.
Connect the critical orifice assembly to the bypass line.
Heat the sampling train.

preconditions.text: The conditions listed in Section 8.1 for direct sampling are met
An analyte is known or suspected to have a concentration above the calibration range or the concentrations of the analytes are not known
The sample extraction system assembly is complete

constraints.text: Care should be taken to avoid the use of fluoropolymer materials.
The bypass pump flow rate must be held constant between 1-2 Lpm to ensure it is not less than the expected sample rate into the canister(s).

steps.text: Perform a post-test leak check.
Disconnect the canister from the train.
Confirm the final canister vacuum.
Attach the vacuum gauge to the canister and open the canister valve.
Record the final pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume (Eq. 19) to the theoretical volume sampled from the sample time and orifice flow rate (Eq. 20).
Close the valve, remove the gauge, and replace the canister inlet cap.
Perform additional measurements, including stack CO2 (if present), stack moisture, and stack flow.
Collect the required data and minimal information for each sample.

constraints.text: A RPD > 20% indicates a potential leak or a clogged orifice and requires resampling.
The pressure should be approximately 17-34 kPa (5-10 in. Hg) vacuum for a 4 to 5 L gas sample collected in the 6 L canister.
If CO2 stack concentration is not available, alternative approaches for measuring CO2 must be performed (e.g., Method 320, plant/facility CEMs, or analysis of Tedlar bag samples).

steps.text: Compare the actual volume (Eq. 19) with the theoretical volume sampled in the field from the sample time and orifice flow rate provided on the COC.
Determine the sampling system background.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.

preconditions.text: The sampling system background sample must be collected in the field prior to the collection of actual samples.

constraints.text: Samples must be analyzed within 30 days.
At least one sampling system background determination must be performed for each test campaign.

steps.text: Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister as described in Section 8.6.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.
Check the field integrity of collected samples by performing the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8.
Flag data that differ between the two approaches by ≥20% RPD and indicate if leakage is suspected, or invalidate the sample with a similar explanation.
Check the laboratory integrity of collected field samples by performing the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 11.1.2.
Flag data that differs between the two approaches by more than 5% RPD and indicate leakage is suspected or invalidate the data with a similar explanation.
Provide COC information, referring to Section 8.6.5 for the minimum information required.
Perform annual gas dilution system evaluations by generating and analyzing blank samples using humidified zero air.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis to verify the accuracy of the system used to dilute calibration standards and samples.

preconditions.text: Upon receipt of the field samples in the laboratory

constraints.text: For high concentration small volume canister samples, volume differences >20% RPD may be acceptable due to differences in pressure measurement equipment.
Gas dilution systems used to generate calibration gases shall be capable of producing standards whose measured values are within ±3 percent of predicted values.
Gas dilution blank samples must meet the blank sample criteria in Table OTM-50-4.

steps.text: Prepare a humidified CCV standard in a canister at a concentration in the lower third of the calibration curve.
Prepare the CCV standard independently from a secondary certified calibration standard (if available).
Purchase and prepare individual targeted VFCs at nominal concentrations from the pure/neat compounds.
Analyze a CCV standard to verify the ICAL for each target compound.
Analyze a daily CCV at the beginning of each analytical sequence.
Perform corrective action such as instrument maintenance, repreparation of the CCV, and recalibration.

preconditions.text: If secondary certified calibration standards are unavailable
If there is failure to meet the ±20% agreement between the quantitated concentrations of the target compounds and the theoretical concentrations

constraints.text: Do not use IS gas standards that fail the LB acceptance criteria.
Each IS compound in each sample injection must be within ±5 seconds of the mean RT for each IS compound in the most recent calibration.
The area response for each IS compound in each injection must be within ±40% of the mean area response of the IS compound determined from the ICAL or the most recent calibration check.
The CCV standard must contain all compounds in the calibration mixture.
Recovery of each target compound in the CCV standard must be within ±20% of the theoretical concentration.
The difference between the quantitated concentrations of the target compounds (%Dccv) and the theoretical concentrations should be within ±20% using Eq. 3 in section 12.
Using the daily CCV to calculate sample concentrations requires the daily CCV VFC RRFs to agree with the ICAL VFC average RRF within ±20% relative percent difference.

steps.text: Evacuate clean passivated stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent, to 50 mTorr.
Inject 50 µL of DI water using a 100-microliter glass displacement syringe.
Prepare VFC calibration standards by diluting individual neat standards and/or from vendor certified pressurized cylinders containing a mixture of the VFC standard targets in nitrogen.
Determine the calibration concentration range needed to span the measured sample concentrations.
Use individual canister calibration standards and the same injection volume to generate calibration curves.
Use either a dynamic or static gas dilution system, or equivalent technique, to blend the VFC calibration gas with zero air.
Allow the pressurized calibration standard canisters to equilibrate at ambient laboratory temperature for a minimum of 12 hours prior to instrumental analysis.

Generate calibration gases by dynamic dilution of a certified compressed gas cylinder containing VFC targets in nitrogen to make a working standard that is diluted with a gas stream of humidified zero air in the heated mixing chamber.

constraints.text: Vendor certified standard gasses must be accurate to 5%.
The VFC calibration standards should be diluted using a scientific grade air or zero air.
The lowest calibration standard used to establish the ICAL must be at least three times the estimated MDL.
Individual canisters must be prepared for each calibration level unless demonstrated that an effective dilution method is consistent with an individual standards calibration described in 9.2.3.1.1.

steps.text: Use the stack equivalent concentration of CO2 determined using an ancillary measurement described in Section 8.6.3 to determine the percentage of CO2 to add to these calibration standards.
Analyze the two standards containing CO2 using the same injection volume that will be used to analyze samples.
Verify if recovery of each target compound in the two standards containing CO2 is within ±30% of the true value for the compound.
Flag the quantitative value for that VFC with an explanation about CO2 interference (see Table OTM-50-4).
Receive canister samples from the field and cross check COC forms with canister identification numbers.
Resolve any disparities before analyzing canisters.

preconditions.text: If the samples are pressurized/diluted prior to analysis, the standards need only to be made to the expected CO2 concentration after pressurization/dilution.

If variable injection volumes are used to generate the calibration curve, the CO2 standards must be prepared in a way to yield the expected concentration at the injection volume used for sample canisters.
If either of the two CO2 spiked canister analyses generate VFC values exceeding ±30% for a given target compound.

constraints.text: Pre-concentrator settings, purge times and/or GC/MS operating conditions may be optimized to decrease the impact of CO2 interference at high concentration levels to avoid invalidating results for coeluting target compounds.

steps.text: Calculate the Average Relative Response for a Target Compound (RRS) using Equation 10.
Calculate the Dilution factor for sample pressurization (DFC) using Equation 11.
Calculate the Relative Response Factor (RRF) using Equation 12.
Calculate the Instrument-Detected Analyte Concentration (CD) using Equation 13.
Calculate the Concentration of the Target Compound in canister as received Stack Gas (Car) using Equation 15.
Calculate the Concentration of the Target Compound in Stack Gas Wet (CSW) using Equation 16.
